meta.disclaimer: This information is for educational purposes only and does not constitute medical advice. Always consult your gastroenterologist before starting, stopping, or changing any medication.

medications.name: Mesalamine
medications.brand: Asacol HD, Pentasa, Lialda, Delzicol, Apriso, Canasa
medications.notes: First-line for mild-to-moderate Crohn's colitis. Less effective for small bowel disease. Requires periodic kidney function monitoring.

medications.name: Sulfasalazine
medications.notes: Most effective for Crohn's colitis. Take with food. Folic acid supplementation recommended (1 mg/day). Avoid in sulfa allergy.

medications.name: Prednisone
medications.notes: For moderate-to-severe flares only. Must taper gradually - never stop abruptly. Not a maintenance therapy. Calcium and vitamin D supplementation recommended during use.

medications.name: Budesonide
sideEffects: Less systemic side effects than prednisone
medications.notes: Topically-acting steroid with fewer systemic effects. Entocort targets ileum and right colon. Not effective for left-sided colonic disease.

medications.name: Methylprednisolone
commonDosages: Dose pack: tapering from 24 mg over 6 days
medications.notes: IV form used for hospitalized patients with severe flares. Oral dose pack sometimes used for short-course treatment.

medications.name: Azathioprine
commonDosages: Start low and titrate up over weeks
medications.notes: Takes 3-6 months for full effect. TPMT/NUDT15 enzyme testing required before starting - determines dosing and safety. Regular blood count and liver function monitoring required (every 1-3 months).

medications.name: 6-Mercaptopurine
medications.notes: Active metabolite of azathioprine. Sometimes tolerated when azathioprine is not. Same TPMT testing and monitoring requirements. Avoid allopurinol unless dose-adjusted.

medications.name: Methotrexate
commonDosages: Oral dosing less reliable due to variable absorption
medications.notes: Injection form preferred over oral for Crohn's. Folic acid 1 mg daily (except methotrexate day) reduces side effects. Absolutely contraindicated in pregnancy - effective contraception required. Avoid alcohol. Regular CBC and liver function tests needed.

medications.name: Infliximab
commonDosages: Dose escalation to 10 mg/kg or every 4 weeks if needed
medications.notes: IV infusion over 2+ hours in a clinic. TB test and hepatitis B screening required before starting. Often combined with an immunomodulator to reduce antibody formation. Therapeutic drug monitoring (trough levels) guides dosing.

medications.name: Adalimumab
medications.notes: Self-injected subcutaneously at home. Pen or prefilled syringe. TB test and hepatitis B screening required. Let pen reach room temperature before injecting to reduce pain. Rotate injection sites.

medications.name: Certolizumab pegol
sideEffects: Same serious risks as other anti-TNFs
medications.notes: PEGylated antibody fragment - does not cross placenta, so preferred anti-TNF in pregnancy. No active Fc region so lower risk of some immune effects. Self-injected.

medications.name: Vedolizumab
commonDosages: SC formulation: 108 mg every 2 weeks after IV induction
medications.notes: Gut-selective - blocks lymphocyte trafficking to gut only. Lower systemic immunosuppression risk. Particularly effective for Crohn's colitis. May take longer to work (up to 14 weeks). No TB test strictly required but often done. SC option available for maintenance.

medications.name: Ustekinumab
commonDosages: Weight-based IV induction: 260 mg (<55 kg), 390 mg (55-85 kg), 520 mg (>85 kg)
medications.notes: First IV dose, then self-injected SC at home. Effective for patients who have failed anti-TNF therapy. Good safety profile. TB screening required.

medications.name: Risankizumab
commonDosages: 360 mg SC at week 12 and every 8 weeks (maintenance)
medications.notes: Selective IL-23 inhibitor (does not block IL-12 like ustekinumab). FDA approved for moderate-to-severe Crohn's in 2022. Strong efficacy data including in anti-TNF failures. Favorable safety profile.

medications.name: Tofacitinib
medications.notes: FDA approved for ulcerative colitis; used off-label in Crohn's. Oral pill - no injections. FDA black box warnings for serious infections, mortality, malignancy, and thrombosis. Avoid in patients >65 or with cardiovascular risk factors.

medications.name: Upadacitinib
sideEffects: Same black box warnings as tofacitinib
medications.notes: FDA approved for Crohn's disease in 2023. Selective JAK1 inhibitor. Oral tablet. Effective in biologic-experienced patients. Same class warnings as tofacitinib. Shingles vaccine recommended before starting.

medications.name: Metronidazole
commonDosages: Typical course: 4-8 weeks for perianal disease
medications.notes: Used for perianal Crohn's disease and abscesses/fistulas. Absolutely avoid alcohol during treatment and 48 hours after. Stop if numbness or tingling develops. Not for long-term use.

medications.name: Ciprofloxacin
medications.notes: Often used with metronidazole for perianal disease. FDA black box warning for tendinitis and tendon rupture - stop at first sign of tendon pain. Avoid in patients taking corticosteroids (increased tendon risk). Take 2 hours before or 6 hours after antacids/iron/calcium.

medications.name: Iron (ferrous sulfate/gluconate/fumarate)
commonDosages: 325 mg ferrous sulfate (65 mg elemental iron) daily
Every other day dosing may improve absorption
IV iron (Infed, Venofer, Feraheme) if oral not tolerated
medications.notes: Iron deficiency anemia is very common in Crohn's. Take with vitamin C to enhance absorption. Avoid taking with dairy, tea, coffee, or calcium. IV iron preferred if oral not tolerated or in active inflammation.

medications.name: Vitamin D (cholecalciferol)
medications.notes: Deficiency extremely common in Crohn's (up to 70%). Important for bone health, immune function, and disease activity. D3 (cholecalciferol) preferred over D2. Check levels at least annually.

medications.name: Vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin/methylcobalamin)
commonDosages: 1000 mcg IM daily for 7 days then weekly for 4 weeks (repletion)
medications.notes: Absorbed in the terminal ileum - deficiency common with ileal disease or resection. Sublingual or IM injection bypasses gut absorption issues. Monitor levels annually, more often after ileal resection.

medications.name: Folic acid
sideEffects: May mask B12 deficiency at high doses
medications.notes: Required supplementation with sulfasalazine and methotrexate. Important for cell division and preventing anemia. Check B12 before starting high-dose folic acid.

medications.name: Calcium (calcium carbonate/citrate)
commonDosages: Calcium citrate preferred if on acid-reducing medications
medications.notes: Essential for bone health, especially with steroid use. Calcium citrate better absorbed than carbonate and does not require stomach acid. Take with vitamin D. Space doses throughout the day (absorb max 500 mg at once). Do not take with iron supplements.

medications.name: Probiotics
commonDosages: Culturelle (L. rhamnosus GG): 1 capsule daily (10 billion CFU)
medications.notes: Evidence strongest for VSL#3 in pouchitis prevention. Limited evidence for Crohn's specifically, but often tried. Discuss with your doctor, especially if immunosuppressed. Saccharomyces boulardii may help prevent C. difficile. Refrigeration required for many products.
